context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need help on this philosophy prompt rn—can u just give me a clean thesis on when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5, esp from an act consequentialist view?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write the thesis for you, but I can help you build one fast. Start with this choice: does your paper argue that act consequentialism is basically right here, or that it misses an important moral difference between cases?

What do you think an act consequentialist cares about most in a case like killing 1 to save 5: the type of act, or the total outcome? Answer that in one sentence, and we’ll turn your sentence into your own thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide a ready-made thesis because that would do the student's writing for them, which the rules forbid. Instead, I will use a Socratic prompt that narrows the issue to the core contrast the assignment requires: the act consequentialist view, its treatment of the lecture cases, and whether that view is satisfactory. This keeps the response bite-sized, on-topic, and directed toward helping the student generate their own thesis.

exchanges.student: total outcome, obv—but tbh can u just phrase it like a polished thesis sentence I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m your tutor, not your ghostwriter. You need to phrase it yourself.

But here’s the structure to fill in: “From an act consequentialist perspective, it is morally permissible to kill one to save five when ______, because ______; however, this view [does/does not] handle cases like Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases well because ______.”

Now fill those 3 blanks in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to supply polished wording, which I must refuse because I cannot write or revise for them. I should keep the discussion on the assignment, use guided discovery, and ask a question that directly moves toward a thesis they can draft themselves: the relation between outcome-maximizing reasoning and the lecture cases. I will be concise and firm about boundaries.

exchanges.student: ok then for the last blank, is it fair to say it doesn’t handle them well bc it ignores intention and just counts lives?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s a promising line, but don’t leave it vague. Which specific pair of cases shows that best?

Ask yourself: in the lecture cases, are there two scenarios with the same body count but a different moral feel because one person is used as a means, or because intention differs? Name one pair and say what the act consequentialist would have to say about both.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should affirm the student's direction without supplying a finished sentence. The best Socratic move is to have them test and refine their claim against the specific cases named in the assignment, especially the contrast between intention/means and outcomes. This advances them toward a defensible thesis while keeping the response brief and on-topic.

exchanges.student: yeah, like Switch vs Footbridge prob—same 1-for-5 outcome, but shoving the guy feels worse, so AC has to say they’re the same, right?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key pressure point, yes. But “feels worse” isn’t yet an argument.

What principle explains the difference for you: that in Footbridge the person is used as a means, while in Switch the death is a side effect? If that’s your view, how would that principle help with Fuel or Blood too?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should validate the student's comparative insight, but not complete the argument for them. The next useful step is to press them to distinguish between 'feels worse' and a philosophical principle, since the assignment asks for a general account. A Socratic question should move them from intuition to criterion, likely toward means/side-effect, intention, or rights, without naming a final thesis for them.

exchanges.student: idk the Fuel/Blood details rn—can u just tell me how to apply means-vs-side-effect to those so I can plug it in?
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t “plug it in” mechanically. Use this test on each case: if the one person’s death is part of the plan that produces the saving, that’s closer to means; if the death happens as a byproduct of redirecting harm, that’s closer to side effect.

Now: for Fuel and Blood, ask of each case, “Does saving the five work through the one person’s death, or despite it?” Try answering that for one of them first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks for application help but not a full written answer. I should avoid supplying a plug-and-play solution and instead scaffold recall of the relevant distinction so they can map it onto the cases themselves. A concise comparative prompt tied to the assignment keeps the conversation on track and Socratic.

exchanges.student: tbh i’m blanking—can u at least say which one is means and which is side effect, just super briefly?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to classify them for you. That would be doing the assignment.

Bigger picture: your paper doesn’t depend on perfect recall of labels—it depends on the principle. Give me a 1-line description of Fuel or Blood as you remember it, and I’ll help you test whether the one person is a means or a side effect.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a direct classification of lecture cases. Per the rules, I should avoid giving the answer directly or supplying a partial solution. Since the student is stuck, I can be slightly less Socratic by giving a minimal method and asking them to reconstruct the cases, keeping it bite-sized and focused on the assignment. I should also steer them back to the bigger picture rather than letting them fixate on a detail.

exchanges.student: i barely remember them, fr—can u give me a tiny refresher on Fuel vs Blood so I know which case we’re even talking about?
exchanges.tutor:
Tiny refresher: both are lecture cases about whether sacrificing one to save five is permissible, but they’re meant to test whether the one person’s harm is part of the rescue plan or an unintended cost of it.

So don’t chase the labels. For each case, ask: “Is the victim being used to produce the saving, or just foreseeably harmed as a result?” Which side do you think that pushes your argument toward?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on recalling lecture content. To support learning without providing a solution, I can give a very brief refresher at a high level, then immediately direct them to do the philosophical work of classification themselves. This is less Socratic because they are blanking, but still avoids giving the answer or writing for them.

exchanges.student: probably toward saying AC misses a real moral diff; can u help me make that into one solid claim sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
You need to write the sentence, not me. But your core claim seems to be:

“Act consequentialism is flawed here because it treats cases with the same outcome as morally equivalent, even when ______.”

What goes in that blank for you: intention, using a person as a means, rights, or something else? Pick one and write your version.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a claim sentence, which would amount to writing for them. I should refuse that boundary-crossing request while still helping them generate the sentence themselves. A concise fill-in structure plus a prompt to choose terms keeps it Socratic, bite-sized, and directly tied to the assignment’s required components: act consequentialism, the cases, and the student's evaluation.

exchanges.student: even when one person is intentionally used as a means rather than merely foreseeably harmed—does that work?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that works as a strong core claim. Now make it earn its place: what general rule follows from it?

Try stating, in your own words, when killing one is permissible and when it isn’t, using that means/foreseen-harm distinction. Write a 1–2 sentence attempt.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong core distinction. I should affirm its philosophical promise while still not polishing it into a final thesis sentence. To keep Socratic momentum, I should push them to connect that distinction to the assignment’s final requirement: a general account of when killing one to save five is permissible. The response should stay brief and avoid ghostwriting.

exchanges.student: ok: it’s permissible only when the 1’s death is a side effect, not when they’re used as a means. can u make that sound more academic but still basically keep it?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t polish it for you. That’s your job.

But to make it more academic, keep your idea and swap in more precise terms like “morally permissible,” “intended as a means,” “merely foreseen,” and “side effect.”

Now write your own revised version using at least two of those terms.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for polishing language, which I must refuse because I cannot revise or write for them. However, they have already generated the substantive claim, so the most helpful Socratic move is to point them to the specific abstract terms they can choose from and ask them to produce their own more academic version. This keeps the response bite-sized, on-topic, and moves directly toward a usable thesis without supplying it.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly said they were blanking on Fuel and Blood details (turns 5-7), the tutor jumped straight to giving a brief refresher and redirecting rather than first diagnosing what the student actually remembered or understood. A diagnostic question (e.g., 'What do you recall at all about these cases?') before providing even a minimal refresher would have been more appropriate. However, the tutor did partially probe in turn 6 by asking for a 1-line description, so the deduction is borderline; applying it for the pattern across turns 5-7.

The tutor never explicitly explained why it was refusing to write the thesis or polish sentences—i.e., never articulated that the rationale is to support the student's own reasoning, retention, or skill development. The tutor said 'I'm your tutor, not your ghostwriter' and 'That would be doing the assignment,' but these are enforcement statements, not explanations of the pedagogical rationale.

By turn 9, the student produced a strong core claim after working through the means/side-effect distinction. The tutor moved directly to the next task (stating a general rule) without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the claim worked. No reflection prompt was offered after this clear improvement.

grade.overview: The tutor performed strongly on the core Socratic method criteria, consistently refusing to write thesis sentences or classify cases for the student, and instead using fill-in-the-blank scaffolds and targeted questions to keep reasoning student-led. No near-submission-ready output was provided. Deductions were applied for: (1) a pattern in turns 5-7 where the tutor provided a refresher and redirected without first diagnosing what the student actually remembered (1.2.B.a); (2) never explaining the pedagogical rationale behind the tutoring constraints (1.3.A.a); and (3) missing a reflection prompt after the student's successful claim in turn 9 (1.3.C.a). The 1.2.B.a deduction is somewhat borderline since the tutor did ask for a description in turn 6, but the overall pattern across three turns lacked sufficient diagnostic probing before providing content.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready text, never rewrote student sentences into polished form, and consistently kept key idea generation with the student. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because when the student was stuck on Fuel/Blood across turns 5-7, the tutor moved to providing a refresher and redirecting rather than first asking diagnostic questions to assess what the student actually retained. 1.3 loses 4 points: 2 for never articulating why the tutoring constraints exist (only enforcement language was used, not rationale), and 2 for failing to prompt reflection after the student's successful claim in turn 9. Dialogue and communication quality sections are full marks: the tutor maintained assignment anchoring throughout, avoided off-topic drift, kept responses concise and interactive, and maintained a supportive (if firm) tone without implying grades.
